location: Overview paragraph 1 vs Stage 2 — Transformation paragraph 1
description: Direct logical contradiction between the Overview's claim that all pipeline stages run in parallel and Stage 2's requirement that Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins. These two statements are mutually exclusive and cannot both be true.
evidence: Overview: 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput.' Stage 2: 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.'
suggestion: Remove the blanket parallelism claim from the Overview. Replace with an accurate description of the execution model: e.g., 'Ingestion and transformation stages operate in a sequential pipeline; the delivery stage begins only after transformation is complete.'

description: The document references 'Section 6' for security considerations, but no Section 6 exists. The document has five sections (Overview, Architecture Components, Performance Characteristics, Operational Notes, Conclusions) and none are numbered.
evidence: 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6.' The document ends at Conclusions — no Section 6 is present and sections are not numbered.
suggestion: Remove the dangling reference. Either add a Security section to the document or replace the sentence with: 'Security considerations are out of scope for this architecture brief and will be addressed in a separate security design document.'

description: The performance claim is factually inverted. 'Batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB' contradicts standard data engineering knowledge: streaming is more efficient at small file sizes because batch processing incurs setup overhead (chunking, buffering, I/O initialization) that yields no benefit at small sizes. The document's own next sentence ('For larger payloads, streaming is preferred') is also inverted — at large payloads, batch processing is typically preferred.
evidence: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB. For larger payloads, streaming is preferred.' Both sentences contradict the standard performance curve and each other.
suggestion: Correct to: 'Internal benchmarks show that streaming is 3× faster than batch for files under 10MB. For larger payloads, batch processing is preferred.'

description: The claim that the in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% is an extraordinary, unsupported assertion. A 99.9% reduction would require going from e.g. 1000ms to 1ms from a single caching layer — implausible without specific benchmark evidence. No baseline, methodology, test conditions, or citation provided.
evidence: 'The in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes.' No supporting data, conditions, or baseline measurement provided.
suggestion: Remove the unsupported percentage. Replace with qualified language: 'The in-memory delivery cache significantly reduces end-to-end write latency compared to unbuffered writes.' If real benchmark data exists, cite it with methodology and conditions.

description: 'Standard retry policy' is referenced but never defined anywhere in the document. A reader cannot implement or configure the system based on this reference alone.
evidence: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' No retry policy (number of attempts, backoff strategy, timeout, failure thresholds) is defined in this document or referenced by name.
suggestion: Define the retry policy inline: e.g., 'applies an exponential backoff retry policy (up to 3 attempts with increasing delay on transient failures)' or reference a specific named document.

description: 'Large files are handled separately' but 'large' is never defined. The threshold between normal and large files is unspecified, making this operationally meaningless.
evidence: 'Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' No size threshold (e.g., '>100MB') is given.
suggestion: Define the threshold: e.g., 'Files exceeding 100MB are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.'

description: 'Pipeline' and 'workflow' are used inconsistently throughout the document — appearing as both synonyms and as apparently distinct concepts — without either term being defined. In Operational Notes, both appear in the same sentence, implying distinction; in Conclusions, they appear as distinct entities ('streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline').
evidence: Operational Notes: 'The pipeline and workflow are monitored by the on-call team via the Prometheus dashboard.' Conclusions: 'adding a real-time streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline.'
suggestion: Choose one term for the system and use it consistently throughout (recommended: 'pipeline'), or explicitly define both: e.g., 'pipeline' refers to the full data processing system, 'workflow' refers to a specific execution mode within it.

description: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB' — the benchmarks are cited but not identified. No benchmark name, methodology, or test conditions are referenced. For a final architecture brief, unattributed performance claims cannot be verified or reproduced.
evidence: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB.' The word 'benchmarks' is used with no attribution, methodology, or conditions.
suggestion: Qualify as internal benchmarks and note conditions, or reference an external source: e.g., 'Internal benchmarks (under [conditions]) show...' This finding is related to F-003 — fixing the factual inversion also requires addressing attribution.

description: The acronym 'ETL' is used on first occurrence without expansion, reducing accessibility for non-specialist readers of this brief.
evidence: 'an internal ETL system that handles batch ingestion, transformation, and delivery' — ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) is not expanded on first use.
suggestion: Expand on first use: 'an internal ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) system'.

description: Authentication is mentioned but the type is not specified. In a final architecture brief, the authentication mechanism is essential implementation information.
evidence: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' No authentication type (API key, OAuth 2.0, mTLS, JWT, etc.) is specified.
suggestion: Specify the authentication mechanism: e.g., 'API key authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' If not yet decided, mark as TBD.
